items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Temple Church
*Country:* scotland
*Region:* edinburgh lothians
*County:* Midlothian
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 14th century
*Coords:* 51.5132, -0.1104
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Temple,_Midlothian
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Temple%20Church%20Midlothian%20scotland
*Main Image:* https://img.castlecore.uk/temple-church.jpg
*Gallery:* 4 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* Update name to Old Temple Kirk , google maps link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/biwFY3CwJ8pGTV1v6 , add source - https://www.templeoldkirk.org/default.asp?page=history , remove wikipedia link as no pages seem to exist 

*Description:*
The Temple Church, a royal peculiar in the Church of England, is a church in the Inner and Middle Temples located between Fleet Street and the River Thames in London, England. It was built by the Knights Templar for their English headquarters in the Temple precinct and consecrated on 10 February 1185 by Patriarch Heraclius of Jerusalem. During the reign of King John (1199–1216) it served as the royal treasury, supported by the role of the Knights Templar as proto-international bankers. It is now jointly owned by the Inner Temple and Middle Temple Inns of Court, bases of the English legal profession. It is famous for being a round church, a common design feature for Knights Templar churches, and for its 13th- and 14th-century stone effigies. It was heavily damaged by German bombing during World War II and has since been greatly restored and rebuilt.

_Listing 4 of 10_

During the reign of King John (1199–1216) it served as the royal treasury, supported by the role of the Knights Templar as proto-international bankers. It is now jointly owned by the Inner Temple and Middle Temple Inns of Court, bases of the English legal profession. It is famous for being a round church, a common design feature for Knights Templar churches, and for its 13th- and 14th-century stone effigies. items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Torphichen Preceptory
*Country:* scotland
*Region:* edinburgh lothians
*County:* West Lothian
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 12th century
*Coords:* 55.93464, -3.6524
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Torphichen_Preceptory
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Torphichen%20Preceptory%20West%20Lothian%20scotland
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Torphichen_Preceptory_5.jpg/330px-Torphichen_Preceptory_5.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Torphichen Preceptory is a church in the village of Torphichen, West Lothian, Scotland. It comprises the remains of the preceptory (headquarters) of the Knights Hospitaller of the Order of St John of Jerusalem in Scotland. The placename may be Gaelic in origin, e.g., "Tóir Féichín" or Brythonic, e.g., modern Welsh "tref fechan". The name "Tóir Féichín" with its Gaelic reference to the Boundary/Sanctuary stones set one mile around the village is most likely the true meaning of the village and Preceptory name rather than the modern Welsh which has no historical precedent that overrides the true meaning of those Neolithic sanctuary stones.

_Listing 1 of 10_

The placename may be Gaelic in origin, e.g., "Tóir Féichín" or Brythonic, e.g., modern Welsh "tref fechan". The name "Tóir Féichín" with its Gaelic reference to the Boundary/Sanctuary stones set one mile around the village is most likely the true meaning of the village and Preceptory name rather than the modern Welsh which has no historical precedent that overrides the true meaning of those Neolithic sanctuary stones.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Loch Leven Castle
*Country:* scotland
*Region:* fife perthshire
*County:* Perth and Kinross
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 14th century
*Coords:* 56.2007614, -3.3917759
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lochleven_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Loch%20Leven%20Castle%20Perth%20and%20Kinross%20scotland
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Lochleven_west_wall.JPG/330px-Lochleven_west_wall.JPG
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Lochleven Castle is a ruined castle on an island in Loch Leven, in the Perth and Kinross local authority area of Scotland. Possibly built around 1300, the castle was the site of military action during the Wars of Scottish Independence (1296–1357). In the latter part of the 14th century, the castle was granted to William Douglas, 1st Earl of Douglas, by his uncle. It remained in the Douglases' hands for the next 300 years. Mary, Queen of Scots, was imprisoned there in 1567–68, and forced to abdicate as queen, before escaping with the help of her gaoler's family. In 1588, the queen's gaoler inherited the title of Earl of Morton, and moved away from the castle. In 1675, Sir William Bruce, an architect, bought the castle and used it as a focal point for his garden; it was never again used as a residence.

_Listing 2 of 10_
